categories.name: OSI Model
categories.description: Test your knowledge of the 7 layers of the OSI reference model

questions.question: Which layer of the OSI model is responsible for end-to-end communication and error-free delivery?
questions.explanation: The Transport Layer (Layer 4) provides end-to-end communication, flow control, and error recovery. TCP and UDP operate at this layer.

questions.question: At which OSI layer do switches primarily operate?
questions.explanation: Switches operate at the Data Link Layer (Layer 2), using MAC addresses to forward frames to the correct port.

questions.question: Which layer is responsible for data encryption and compression?
questions.explanation: The Presentation Layer (Layer 6) handles data translation, encryption, and compression between the application and network.

questions.question: What is the primary function of the Network Layer?
questions.explanation: The Network Layer (Layer 3) handles logical addressing (IP addresses) and determines the best path for data routing.

questions.question: Which PDU (Protocol Data Unit) is used at the Transport Layer?
questions.explanation: The Transport Layer uses 'Segments' (TCP) or 'Datagrams' (UDP). The PDU hierarchy is: Bits → Frames → Packets → Segments → Data.

questions.question: The Session Layer is responsible for which of the following?
questions.explanation: The Session Layer (Layer 5) manages sessions between applications — it handles setup, maintenance, and teardown of communication sessions.

questions.question: Which OSI layer defines electrical signals, cable types, and physical topology?
questions.explanation: The Physical Layer (Layer 1) defines the hardware elements — cables, voltages, pin layouts, and data rates.

questions.question: At which layer does the HTTP protocol operate?
questions.explanation: HTTP operates at the Application Layer (Layer 7), providing web browsing functionality directly to end users.

categories.name: TCP/IP
categories.description: Questions about TCP, UDP, IP, and the TCP/IP protocol suite

questions.question: What is the purpose of the TCP three-way handshake?
questions.explanation: The three-way handshake (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK) establishes a reliable TCP connection and synchronizes sequence numbers between client and server.

questions.question: Which flag combination is used in the second step of the TCP three-way handshake?
questions.explanation: The server responds with SYN+ACK — acknowledging the client's SYN and sending its own synchronization request.

questions.question: What is the maximum size of a TCP segment's data payload (MSS) typically set to?
questions.explanation: The typical MSS is 1460 bytes — derived from the standard MTU of 1500 bytes minus 20 bytes for IP header and 20 bytes for TCP header.

questions.question: Which protocol is connectionless and does not guarantee delivery?
questions.explanation: UDP (User Datagram Protocol) is connectionless — it sends data without establishing a connection and does not guarantee delivery, ordering, or duplicate protection.

questions.question: What does TCP use to handle congestion in the network?
options: Congestion window and slow start algorithm
questions.explanation: TCP uses a congestion window (cwnd) and algorithms like Slow Start, Congestion Avoidance, Fast Retransmit, and Fast Recovery to manage network congestion.

questions.question: What is the size of an IPv4 address?
questions.explanation: IPv4 addresses are 32 bits (4 bytes), typically represented in dotted-decimal notation like 192.168.1.1. IPv6 addresses are 128 bits.

questions.question: What does the TTL field in an IP packet prevent?
questions.explanation: TTL (Time to Live) is decremented by each router. When it reaches 0, the packet is discarded — preventing infinite loops in case of routing errors.

questions.question: In TCP, what triggers a fast retransmit?
questions.explanation: Fast Retransmit is triggered when the sender receives 3 duplicate ACKs for the same segment, indicating packet loss without waiting for a timeout.

categories.name: Subnetting
categories.description: Practice subnetting, CIDR notation, and IP address calculations

questions.question: What is the subnet mask for a /24 network?
questions.explanation: /24 means 24 bits for the network portion, which translates to 255.255.255.0 (three full octets masked).

questions.question: How many usable host addresses are in a /28 subnet?
questions.explanation: /28 = 32 - 28 = 4 host bits = 2^4 = 16 addresses. Subtract 2 (network and broadcast) = 14 usable hosts.

questions.question: What is the network address of 192.168.10.67/26?
questions.explanation: /26 means blocks of 64. 67 falls in the 64-127 block, so the network address is 192.168.10.64.

questions.question: Which of the following is a private IP address range?
questions.explanation: 172.16.0.0 to 172.31.255.255 is one of three private IP ranges (RFC 1918). The others are 10.0.0.0/8 and 192.168.0.0/16.

questions.question: What is CIDR and what problem does it solve?
options: A routing protocol that replaces OSPF
A notation system that replaces classful addressing for efficient IP allocation
questions.explanation: CIDR (Classless Inter-Domain Routing) allows variable-length subnet masks, replacing the wasteful Class A/B/C system for more efficient IP allocation.

questions.question: What is the broadcast address of the subnet 10.0.0.0/22?
questions.explanation: /22 gives 2^(32-22) = 1024 addresses. Starting at 10.0.0.0, the range ends at 10.0.3.255 (broadcast address).

categories.name: DNS
categories.description: Domain Name System resolution, record types, and caching

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of DNS?
options: Translating domain names to IP addresses
questions.explanation: DNS (Domain Name System) translates human-readable domain names (like google.com) into IP addresses (like 142.250.80.46) that computers use.

questions.question: Which DNS record type maps a domain name to an IPv4 address?
questions.explanation: An 'A' record maps a domain to an IPv4 address. 'AAAA' is for IPv6, 'CNAME' is an alias, and 'MX' is for mail servers.

questions.question: What is the correct order of DNS resolution (recursive query)?
options: Browser → Recursive Resolver → Root → TLD → Authoritative
questions.explanation: A full DNS resolution follows: Browser cache → Recursive Resolver → Root Server → TLD Server → Authoritative Server, each step narrowing down the domain.

questions.question: What does TTL mean in the context of DNS records?
options: Time to Live — how long a record is cached
questions.explanation: TTL (Time to Live) in DNS specifies how long (in seconds) a DNS record should be cached by resolvers before requesting a fresh copy.

questions.question: Which DNS record type is used for email server routing?
questions.explanation: MX (Mail Exchange) records specify the mail servers responsible for receiving email for a domain, along with priority values.

questions.question: What port does DNS typically use?
questions.explanation: DNS uses port 53 for both UDP (standard queries) and TCP (zone transfers and large responses).

categories.name: DHCP
categories.description: Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol and IP address assignment

questions.question: What does DHCP stand for?
questions.explanation: DHCP stands for Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol — it automatically assigns IP addresses and network configuration to devices on a network.

questions.question: What is the correct order of the DHCP DORA process?
questions.explanation: DORA: Discover (client broadcasts), Offer (server offers IP), Request (client requests offered IP), Acknowledge (server confirms assignment).

questions.question: What transport protocol and port does DHCP use?
questions.explanation: DHCP uses UDP — the server listens on port 67 and the client listens on port 68. UDP is used because the client may not have an IP yet.

questions.question: What is a DHCP lease?
options: A temporary IP assignment with an expiration time
questions.explanation: A DHCP lease is a temporary assignment of an IP address. The client must renew the lease before it expires to keep the same IP.

questions.question: What happens during a DHCP Discover message?
options: Server sends an IP to a specific client
Client broadcasts to find available DHCP servers
Server removes an IP from the pool
questions.explanation: DHCP Discover is a broadcast message (255.255.255.255) sent by a client to find any available DHCP servers on the network.

questions.question: What additional information can DHCP provide besides an IP address?
options: Subnet mask, default gateway, DNS servers, and lease duration
questions.explanation: DHCP can provide: IP address, subnet mask, default gateway, DNS servers, domain name, lease duration, and other network parameters.

categories.name: Routing
categories.description: Routing protocols, algorithms, and path selection

questions.question: What is the difference between static and dynamic routing?
options: Static uses algorithms, dynamic is manual
Static is manually configured, dynamic uses routing protocols
questions.explanation: Static routing requires manual configuration of routes. Dynamic routing uses protocols (OSPF, BGP, RIP) to automatically discover and update routes.

questions.question: Which routing protocol uses hop count as its metric and has a maximum of 15 hops?
questions.explanation: RIP (Routing Information Protocol) uses hop count as its metric with a maximum of 15 hops. 16 hops = unreachable. This limits RIP to smaller networks.

questions.question: What type of routing protocol is BGP?
questions.explanation: BGP (Border Gateway Protocol) is an Exterior Gateway Protocol used for routing between autonomous systems (AS) on the internet.

questions.question: What algorithm does OSPF use to calculate the shortest path?
questions.explanation: OSPF uses Dijkstra's Shortest Path First (SPF) algorithm to calculate the best route to each destination in its link-state database.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a default route (0.0.0.0/0)?
options: Route packets when no specific match exists in the routing table
questions.explanation: The default route (0.0.0.0/0) is a catch-all — it forwards packets to a next-hop gateway when no more specific route matches in the routing table.

questions.question: What is administrative distance in routing?
options: A value indicating the trustworthiness of a routing source
questions.explanation: Administrative distance is a value (0-255) that rates the trustworthiness of routing information. Lower = more trusted. Directly connected = 0, Static = 1, OSPF = 110, RIP = 120.

categories.name: Switching
categories.description: Layer 2 switching, VLANs, STP, and MAC address tables

questions.question: What does a network switch use to forward frames?
questions.explanation: Switches operate at Layer 2 and use MAC (Media Access Control) addresses to make forwarding decisions using their MAC address table.

questions.question: What is a VLAN?
options: A virtual local area network that logically segments a physical network
questions.explanation: VLANs (Virtual LANs) logically divide a physical switch into separate broadcast domains, improving security and performance.

questions.question: What is the purpose of Spanning Tree Protocol (STP)?
questions.explanation: STP prevents broadcast storms and loops in networks with redundant Layer 2 paths by blocking certain ports to create a loop-free topology.

questions.question: What happens when a switch receives a frame for an unknown MAC address?
options: Floods the frame to all ports except the source
Sends it to the default gateway
questions.explanation: When the destination MAC is not in the MAC table, the switch floods (forwards) the frame out all ports except the one it was received on.

questions.question: What is a trunk port on a switch?
options: A port that connects to end devices
A port that carries traffic for multiple VLANs between switches
A port used only for management
A port that blocks all traffic
questions.explanation: Trunk ports carry traffic for multiple VLANs using tagging (802.1Q). They connect switches together and preserve VLAN membership across devices.

questions.question: Which IEEE standard defines VLAN tagging?
questions.explanation: IEEE 802.1Q defines VLAN tagging — it adds a 4-byte tag to Ethernet frames to identify which VLAN the frame belongs to.
